questions.text: Who is known as the 'Father of Genetics'?
questions.explanation: Gregor Johann Mendel conducted hybridization experiments on garden peas and proposed the laws of inheritance.

questions.text: Mendel selected how many pairs of contrasting characters in pea plants?
questions.explanation: Mendel studied 7 pairs of contrasting traits such as plant height, seed color, and flower position.

questions.text: The term 'allele' refers to:
questions.explanation: Alleles are slightly different forms of the same gene located at the same locus on homologous chromosomes.

questions.text: What is the phenotypic ratio of a Mendelian monohybrid cross in F2 generation?
questions.explanation: In a cross between homozygous dominant and recessive parents, the F2 offspring show a 3:1 ratio of dominant to recessive traits.

questions.text: A cross used to determine whether a dominant phenotype is homozygous or heterozygous is called:
questions.explanation: A test cross involves crossing an individual with a dominant phenotype with a homozygous recessive individual.

questions.text: Incomplete dominance is seen in which of the following?
questions.explanation: In Snapdragon, a cross between red and white flowers produces pink flowers (F1).

questions.text: ABO blood grouping in humans is an example of:
questions.explanation: ABO groups involve three alleles (I^A, I^B, i) and show co-dominance in the AB phenotype.

questions.text: The chromosomal theory of inheritance was proposed by:
questions.explanation: Walter Sutton and Theodore Boveri noted that the behavior of chromosomes was parallel to the behavior of genes.

questions.text: Who provided experimental verification for the chromosomal theory of inheritance?
questions.explanation: Morgan used Drosophila melanogaster to prove that genes are located on chromosomes.

questions.text: The tendency of genes located on the same chromosome to be inherited together is called:
questions.explanation: Linkage describes the physical association of genes on a chromosome.

questions.text: In XO type of sex determination, females have:
questions.explanation: In many insects like grasshoppers, females are XX and males are XO.

questions.text: Male heterogamety is found in:
questions.explanation: In humans, males produce two types of gametes (X and Y), while females produce one type (X).

questions.text: Which of the following disorders is caused by a point mutation?
questions.explanation: Sickle-cell anemia is due to a single base substitution in the beta-globin chain of hemoglobin.

questions.text: Hemophilia is a ________ disorder.
questions.explanation: Hemophilia is a Mendelian disorder linked to the X-chromosome, mostly affecting males.

questions.text: A person with Down's syndrome has ________ chromosomes.
questions.explanation: Down's syndrome is caused by the trisomy of chromosome 21 (total 47 chromosomes).

questions.text: Klinefelter's syndrome is characterized by the genotype:
questions.explanation: Affected individuals are males with an extra X chromosome, showing feminine features like gynaecomastia.

questions.text: Gynaecomastia is a symptom of:
questions.explanation: Gynaecomastia refers to the development of breast tissue in males.

questions.text: Turner's syndrome occurs due to:
questions.explanation: Females with Turner's syndrome lack one X chromosome (45, XO) and are sterile.

questions.text: Thalassemia is an example of:
questions.explanation: Thalassemia involves a reduced rate of synthesis of globin chains (quantitative problem).

questions.text: A family tree analysis used to study the inheritance of a particular trait is called:
questions.explanation: Pedigree analysis is used to trace traits through multiple generations in humans.

questions.text: Mendel's Law of Segregation is based on the fact that:
options: Alleles do not show any blending
Gametes receive only one of the two factors
questions.explanation: Alleles separate during gamete formation so that a gamete carries only one allele for a trait.

questions.text: Which law states that factors for different traits are distributed to gametes independently?
questions.explanation: This law applies to genes on different chromosomes or far apart on the same chromosome.

questions.text: The F2 phenotypic ratio of a dihybrid cross is:
questions.explanation: 9 (both dominant) : 3 (one dom.) : 3 (other dom.) : 1 (both recessive).

questions.text: When a single gene influences multiple phenotypic traits, it is called:
questions.explanation: Phenylketonuria is an example where a single gene defect leads to mental retardation and skin pigmentation changes.

questions.text: Human skin color is an example of:
questions.explanation: Traits controlled by three or more genes show a gradient of phenotypes.

questions.text: In birds, the females are ________ and males are ________.
questions.explanation: In birds, female heterogamety is observed (ZW).

questions.text: A man with blood group A marries a woman with blood group B. What are the possible blood groups of their children if both parents are heterozygous?
questions.explanation: Crossing I^A i and I^B i can produce all four genotypes/phenotypes.

questions.text: Distance between genes is measured in:
questions.explanation: One centimorgan (cM) is the unit of map distance, representing 1% recombination frequency.

questions.text: Mutations are:
questions.explanation: Mutations are the source of new genetic variation in a population.

questions.text: Physical or chemical agents that induce mutations are called:
questions.explanation: Examples include UV radiation, X-rays, and mustard gas.

questions.text: Color blindness is more common in males because:
options: It is an X-linked recessive trait
questions.explanation: Males have only one X chromosome, so a single recessive allele causes the disorder.

questions.text: In pedigree analysis, a solid (filled) circle represents a/an:
questions.explanation: Circles are females, squares are males. Filled symbols denote the presence of the trait.

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT a Mendelian disorder?
questions.explanation: Down's syndrome is a chromosomal disorder caused by aneuploidy, not a single gene defect.

questions.text: The failure of segregation of chromatids during cell division resulting in gain or loss of a chromosome is:
questions.explanation: Aneuploidy includes conditions like trisomy and monosomy.

questions.text: An increase in a whole set of chromosomes in an organism is called:
questions.explanation: This is often seen in plants after failure of cytokinesis.

questions.text: Which scientist first observed the 'X-body' in insects?
questions.explanation: Henking (1891) traced a specific nuclear structure through spermatogenesis in insects.

questions.text: Phenylketonuria is an inborn error of metabolism inherited as an:
questions.explanation: Affected individuals lack an enzyme that converts phenylalanine into tyrosine.

questions.text: What is the reason for the 'blending' seen in F1 of a cross between red and white snapdragons?
questions.explanation: The dominant allele is not completely dominant over the recessive allele.

questions.text: The number of linkage groups in an organism corresponds to its:
questions.explanation: Since genes on one chromosome belong to one linkage group, the number equals the number of pairs of chromosomes.

questions.text: Starch grain size in pea seeds shows:
questions.explanation: The B gene controls both starch synthesis and seed shape; grain size shows incomplete dominance (Bb is intermediate).
